records.culinary_uses: Young tender tips can be cooked as a spring green or steeped fresh or dried for tea.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional lymphatic and urinary support; cooling herb often used as a fresh infusion.
records.notes: Low sprawling annual with whorled leaves, rough sticky stems, and narrow lance-shaped pointed leaves. Tiny white flowers followed by clinging burr-like seeds. Often found in rich disturbed ground, hedgerows, moist edges, and thickets.

records.culinary_uses: Young spring shoots can be peeled lightly and cooked like asparagus or added to mixed greens when still tender.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional soothing tea plant used for mild digestive and inflammatory complaints.
records.notes: Tall plant of burns, clearings, roadsides, and sunny edges. Leaves alternate, narrow, and pointed. Showy pink flowers in summer.

records.culinary_uses: Tender inner shoots can be eaten raw or cooked and have a mild cucumber-like to green-corn flavor.
records.medicinal_uses: Limited folk topical use; more of a food than a medicine.
records.notes: Wetland plant with long blade-like leaves arising in dense basal fans. Found in marshes, ponds, and slow-water margins.

records.culinary_uses: Very young shoots can be boiled in changes of water and eaten as a cooked green by people familiar with proper preparation.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional medicinal use exists, but this is a plant that needs careful handling and preparation knowledge.
records.notes: Opposite leaves when young, smooth stems, white latex sap, and later pink flower clusters. Use only with strong confidence in identification and preparation.

records.culinary_uses: Leaves can be used fresh or dried for tea, seasoning, desserts, and savory dishes.
records.medicinal_uses: Common tea herb used for mild digestive support and soothing aromatic infusions.
records.notes: Mint-family plant with opposite leaves, square stems, and strong mint aroma when crushed. Often near damp ground.

records.culinary_uses: Leaves are strongly flavored and best used sparingly as seasoning or tea rather than as a bulk vegetable.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional wound, fever, and digestive herb with a long history of use.
records.notes: Finely divided feathery leaves, alternate arrangement, and flat-topped white flower clusters. Aromatic when crushed.

records.culinary_uses: Fruits can be used for tea, syrup, jelly, and puree once the irritating inner hairs and seeds are handled carefully.
records.medicinal_uses: Vitamin-C-rich traditional winter tea and tonic fruit.
records.notes: Shrubby rose with prickly stems, compound leaves, pink flowers, and red to orange hips in late season.

records.culinary_uses: Early spring leaves can be cooked as pungent greens; bitterness softens with blanching or sautéing.
records.medicinal_uses: Minor traditional spring tonic use.
records.notes: Basal rosette in spring with lobed shiny leaves and later yellow mustard-family flowers.

records.culinary_uses: Very young peeled shoots and leaf stalks have been cooked traditionally, but this plant demands careful identification and skin protection.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional uses exist, but food use should be cautious due to look-alikes and sap sensitivity.
records.notes: Large coarse plant with huge compound leaves, thick hollow stems, and broad white umbrella-shaped flower clusters. Sap can irritate sun-exposed skin.

records.culinary_uses: Classic spring fiddlehead, best thoroughly cooked. Flavor is often compared to green beans or asparagus.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a food plant rather than a medicinal one.
records.notes: Smooth green fiddleheads with a deep U-shaped groove on the inside of the stalk. Found in moist rich bottoms and river edges.

records.culinary_uses: Leaves can be used fresh or dried for tea and aromatic seasoning.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional tea herb for colds, mild sore throat, and general aromatic support.
records.notes: Mint-family plant with opposite leaves, square stems, and lavender to pink shaggy flower heads.

records.culinary_uses: Seeds can be dried and added in small amounts to foods, granola, or herb blends.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional tonic use in small quantities.
records.notes: Produced on nettle plants with opposite pointed leaves and stinging hairs. Harvest only after positive identification and with gloves.

records.culinary_uses: Leached acorns can be dried and ground into flour for breads, pancakes, mush, or blended baking.
records.medicinal_uses: Primarily a food resource.
records.notes: Oak nuts require proper leaching to remove tannins. Trees have alternate lobed leaves and acorns in caps.

records.culinary_uses: Tender heart-shaped young leaves are mild and can be eaten fresh in salads or sandwiches.
records.medicinal_uses: Flowers are more often medicinally used, but the tree is broadly useful.
records.notes: Tree with alternate heart-shaped leaves, soft texture when young, and fragrant pale flowers later in season.

records.culinary_uses: Berry clusters can be soaked in cool water to make a tart lemonade-like drink.
records.medicinal_uses: Astringent traditional use exists, but this is mostly a drink ingredient.
records.notes: Large red upright berry clusters on shrubs or small trees with alternate compound leaves. Avoid white-berried poison sumac.

records.culinary_uses: Historically cooked in careful preparations, but this is not a casual beginner plant and is better treated cautiously.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional use exists, but it is not a carefree edible.
records.notes: Wetland plant with glossy rounded to kidney-shaped leaves and bright yellow buttercup-like flowers in spring.

records.culinary_uses: Popular firm bracket mushroom often cooked like chicken when young and tender.
records.medicinal_uses: Primarily a culinary species.
records.notes: Large bright orange to yellow shelf fungus on wood, often overlapping in clusters. No true gills underneath.

records.culinary_uses: Choice late-season mushroom with layered gray-brown fronds and rich texture.
records.medicinal_uses: Also studied and used in medicinal extracts.
records.notes: Large clustered polypore at the base of hardwoods, especially oak. Fronded, leafy appearance.

records.culinary_uses: White shaggy tooth fungus with seafood-like texture when cooked.
records.medicinal_uses: Widely used in modern medicinal mushroom products and research.
records.notes: White cascading spines hanging from a single mass on hardwood. Distinct tooth fungus.

records.culinary_uses: Edible white tooth fungus similar in use to lion's mane.
records.medicinal_uses: Related tooth fungi are used medicinally as well as culinarily.
records.notes: Branching white mass with hanging icicle-like teeth on hardwood logs.

records.culinary_uses: Edible when the interior is pure white and firm, often sliced and fried.
records.medicinal_uses: Minor folk topical use; mostly a food species.
records.notes: Huge round white puffball in fields or edges. Interior must be solid white with no developing mushroom shape.

records.culinary_uses: Good edible while young and white before the cap begins turning black and inky.
records.medicinal_uses: Primarily a culinary species.
records.notes: Tall white shaggy cylindrical cap that quickly deliquesces into black ink as it ages.

records.culinary_uses: Choice mushroom with rich smoky aroma; excellent dried.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a culinary mushroom.
records.notes: Dark funnel-shaped mushroom with hollow center, often hidden among leaf litter in hardwood forests.

records.culinary_uses: Classic choice mushroom with fruity aroma and blunt ridges instead of true gills.
records.medicinal_uses: Primarily culinary.
records.notes: Yellow to orange funneling mushrooms in forest soil with false gills that run down the stem.

records.culinary_uses: Good edible with mild flavor and distinctive soft teeth under the cap.
records.medicinal_uses: Mainly a culinary species.
records.notes: Pale buff to orange cap with brittle tooth-like spines underneath rather than gills.

records.culinary_uses: Edible ruffled mushroom with noodle-like texture after thorough cleaning.
records.medicinal_uses: Some medicinal interest exists, but it is chiefly culinary.
records.notes: Large cream to yellow curly mass at the base of conifers, looking like a brainy cauliflower.

records.culinary_uses: Parasitized honey mushroom clusters valued by some hunters for dense texture and savory flavor.
records.medicinal_uses: Culinary species more than medicinal.
records.notes: Dense pale clustered growth associated with aborted entoloma and honey mushroom habitats.

records.culinary_uses: Highly prized bolete with thick stem, brown cap, and pores instead of gills.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly culinary.
records.notes: Robust mushroom with sponge-like underside and a white netted upper stem. Avoid red-pored toxic boletes.

records.culinary_uses: Edible bolete often peeled before cooking because of the slimy cap skin.
records.medicinal_uses: Primarily culinary.
records.notes: Sticky brown cap, yellow pores, and frequent association with pines.

records.culinary_uses: Classic field Agaricus edible with pink to chocolate-brown gills when mature.
records.medicinal_uses: Culinary species.
records.notes: White cap in grassy areas with free gills that start pink and darken. Careful separation from poisonous white Amanitas is essential.

records.culinary_uses: Typically used as tea or extract rather than as a fleshy food mushroom.
records.medicinal_uses: One of the best-known medicinal polypores.
records.notes: Thin zoned multicolored bracket on dead hardwood, velvety above with tiny pores beneath.

records.culinary_uses: Edible late-season mushroom with lilac tones and dense texture when correctly identified and cooked.
records.medicinal_uses: Primarily culinary.
records.notes: Lilac to tan mushroom of leaf litter and composty woods, often with a fragrant fruity odor.

records.culinary_uses: Edible shelf-like mushroom on hardwoods, though not a true oyster mushroom.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly culinary.
records.notes: Pale fan-shaped caps on hardwood trunks with a stem and true gills; often on elm or box elder.
